content.assessment: Networked retention avoids the binary of closure-versus-status-quo that drives much of the segregation dynamic, and the multi-service hub model could specifically counter the pattern where village schools transferred to church maintainers serve more advantaged intakes (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04) by keeping public options viable through resource pooling rather than transfer. However, the evidence base for this lens raises concern that success in the one documented Hungarian case depended on rare local social capital (implicitly referenced in the proposal's own risk statement), suggesting equity outcomes under this model could be highly uneven across villages with differing organizational capacity, again potentially concentrating benefit away from the most disadvantaged settlements in North-East Hungary where ghetto schools are already concentrated (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05).
opportunities: Formal networked structures could substitute for organic social capital, potentially extending success beyond isolated cases to structurally disadvantaged villages that lack such capital
Clustering could counter the territorial concentration of ghetto schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05) by pooling resources across settlements rather than allowing isolated decline
strengths: Offers an alternative to church-transfer that could preserve public, more socioeconomically diverse intake compared to the documented advantaged intake of church schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
Kindergarten co-location addresses access gaps at an earlier stage, potentially improving downstream mobility given documented compounding disadvantage (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
threats: Informal inter-municipal competition for pupils could undermine cooperation specifically among villages competing for the same shrinking pool of disadvantaged students, worsening rather than easing sorting
Absent dedicated capital investment, hub conversion may occur disproportionately in villages with the fiscal or social capacity to organize it, replicating unequal outcomes
weaknesses: Reliance on local social capital as a determining success factor risks reproducing unequal outcomes across villages with unequal organizational resources, similar to the pattern already visible between advantaged and disadvantaged school populations (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)

Does not directly address teacher shortage and quality gaps concentrated in rural/disadvantaged schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09), which may undermine hub viability regardless of networking structure
